observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
a. City Administrator to prepare a memorandum requiring all department heads to institute proper planning, scheduling and strategic implementation of PPAs and strict adherence to the granting and liquidation of cash advances pursuant to COA Circular No. 97-002 and Section 89 of PD No. 1445.
b. City Accountant and City Treasurer to:
● Send updated demand letters to all AOs with unliquidated cash advances; and
● Stop the practice of granting additional or new cash advances to AOs with outstanding cash advances unless their previous cash advances are fully liquidated.
We also recommended that the City Mayor: a. Consider filing appropriate actions against AOs who have more than two outstanding cash advances or whose cash advances are over a year old; and b. Issue a memorandum requiring all AOs and concerned officials to strictly comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 to ensure proper accountability and timely liquidation of cash advances.
observations.managementAction:
A memorandum dated April 15, 2024 was prepared and issued by the City Administrator requiring all department heads to institute proper planning, scheduling and strategic implementation of PPAs and strict adherence to the granting and liquidation of cash advances pursuant to COA Circular No. 97-002 and Section 89 of PD No. 1445.
Demand letters were given to AOs.
The balances for cash advances were reduced for CY 2024.
The City Accountant initially identified those AOs who failed to settle their long and overdue cash advances but no actions yet were undertaken as prescribed in COA Circular No. 97-002.
observations.reason:
Implemented: A memorandum dated April 15, 2024 was prepared and issued; demand letters were given to AOs; balances reduced for CY 2024.
Not Implemented: Resorting to cash advance was still practiced due to urgent needs. No action was taken to file administrative/criminal sanctions as warranted. Reiterated in Audit Observation No. 1, Page Nos. 36-41, Part II of this Report. For preparation of RTS for recommendations considered closed for having been reiterated with modifications.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to:
a. Provide Allowance for Impairment for the assessed/non-collectible receivables, in compliance with the pertinent provisions of IPSAS 29; and
b. Facilitate request for write-off and conduct necessary procedures for proper disposition of dormant accounts as prescribed in Item 7.0 of COA Circular No. 2023-008.
observations.managementAction: Allowance for impairment was provided.
observations.reason:
Implemented: Allowance for impairment was provided.
Not Implemented: Documents needed as support for a request for write-off was being collated. Few individuals who were accountables were already deceased. However, there is still no initial steps taken for the filing of authority to write-off.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to:
a. Identify recording errors by tracing back all transactions from CYs 2015 to 2023 affecting the Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds accounts and effect the necessary adjustments to correct the reported balances of the reciprocal accounts; and
b. Ensure that Transactions which affect two or more funds are recorded simultaneously, using the proper reciprocal accounts, in the corresponding books of accounts to prevent the incurrence of unreconciled differences, in compliance with Section 111 of PD No. 1445 and the RCA for LGUs.
observations.managementAction: Initial tracing was undertaken. Transactions were recorded simultaneously.
observations.reason:
Not Implemented: Tracing of records takes back all time to complete.
Implemented: Transactions were recorded simultaneously.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the concerned contractors to immediately rectify the noted deficiencies; otherwise, the relevant transactions will be suspended in audit and ensure that necessary supporting documents are submitted simultaneously with the copy of perfected contracts to facilitate timely and systematic review and evaluation of the projects.
observations.managementAction: No action was taken. The City Engineering Office stated it had relayed the Audit Observation Memorandum to the BAC, recognizing it as the more appropriate body to facilitate the submission of the necessary documents for the 17 infrastructure projects.
observations.reason: Some documents were inadvertently not submitted. Reiterated in Audit Observation No. 6, Page Nos. 52-54, Part II of this Report. For preparation of RTS for recommendations considered closed for having been reiterated with modifications.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the concerned contractors to immediately rectify the noted deficiencies; otherwise, demand refund of the equivalent excessive cost thereof. We also recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Engineer to ensure that the approved Program of Works (POW) and Detailed Estimates of any project are not overestimated and/or do not include unnecessary items/works to prevent the incurrence of excessive ABC and contract cost, in compliance with Section 36 of RA No. 9184.
observations.managementAction: Instructions were given to concerned officials. Notice of Disallowance (ND) Nos. 24-001-101-(22-24), 24-002-101-(22-24) and 24-003-101-(23) were issued to Management last September 2024.
observations.reason: Not Implemented: Some contractors did not rectify noted deficiencies; Notices of Disallowance were issued. Implemented: Instructions were given to concerned officials.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the BAC Chairperson to ensure that copies of the contracts and all their essential supporting documents are furnished to the Office of the Auditor within five working days from their execution, in compliance with COA Circular No. 2009-001.
observations.managementAction: The Chairperson of the BAC, in a letter dated February 28, 2025, explained that the noted contracts were inadvertently missed for submission to the Office of the Auditor. Nevertheless, they submitted certified true copies of the contracts listed in the audit observation.
observations.reason: Not Implemented: Initially missing. Implemented: Certified true copies later submitted; some contracts were overlooked.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor, in coordination with the Sangguniang Panlungsod, expedite the preparation of the documents relative to the noted donations. We also recommended that the City Mayor direct the: a. Appraisal and Disposal Committee to provide the fair market values or appraisal values of the donated property if there are no declared values in the donation documents; b. City General Services Officer to issue Property Acknowledgement Receipt (PAR)/Acknowledgement Receipt of Equipment (ARE) and install property stickers for the donated property; c. City Accountant to recognize the donated property in the books of accounts upon receipt of the donation documents; and d. Concerned offices to ensure proper coordination are in place from receipt until appropriate recording of the donated property to ensure appropriate recognition and property accountability.
observations.managementAction:
Fair Market Value was provided by the Committee.
PAR was issued and property stickers were installed to the donated properties.
Donated property was recorded under JEV No. 101-2024-03-005.
The City Administrator issued a memorandum to ensure timely and appropriate recognition of donated properties.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the: a. City GSO to submit a complete Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) for all unserviceable properties; and b. Appraisal and Disposal Committee to facilitate the immediate disposal of the noted unserviceable properties in accordance with the existing rules and regulations; and c. City Accountant to draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to drop from the books the sold/condemned property upon disposal.
observations.managementAction: The City GSO made a reassessment of the properties. The City GSO stated that upon reviewing the operational capacity, some were still in use while others could be utilized after repairs. They are evaluating remaining properties and will submit updated IIRUP once assessment completed.
observations.reason:
Not Implemented: No action was taken to submit a complete IIRUP and complete disposal. A discrepancy remains between IIRUP and RPCPPE. Reiterated with modifications in Audit Observation No. 11, Page Nos. 61-63, Part II of this Report. For preparation of RTS for recommendations considered closed for having been reiterated with modifications.
Partially Implemented: Preliminary disposal proceedings ongoing; appraisal of motor vehicles and machinery being undertaken.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) Chairperson to evaluate the proper mode of procurement to attain the most practical, economical and advantageous mode for the procuring entity, in compliance with the pertinent provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 and ensure that copies of properly filled-out POs and its supporting documents are furnished to the Office of the Auditor within five working days from its execution, in compliance with COA Circular No. 2009-001.
observations.managementAction: POs were properly filled-out and copies were furnished to the Office of the Auditor, along with the supporting documents.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated that the City Mayor enforce withholding of the salary of the then Disbursing Officer with unliquidated cash advances without prejudice to instituting other sanctions pursuant to COA Circular No. 97-002 and direct the City Accountant and City Treasurer to: a. Facilitate the prompt return of the unexpended balances to TESDA Regional Office No. IV-A; and b. Ensure that unexpended balances of fund transfers from completed programs are returned promptly to the source agencies.
observations.managementAction: Verification with the Regional Accountant of TESDA was conducted on October 3, 2024, during which it was agreed that the amount of P732,053.68 would be returned to TESDA following a refund by the accountable officer who initially drew the cash advance.
observations.reason: Not Implemented: No withholding of salary done; no full return yet. For preparation of RTS for recommendations considered closed for having been reiterated with modifications.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City General Services Officer to immediately facilitate the insurance of the properties with the GSIS under the Government Service Insurance Fund. We also recommended the City Mayor, in coordination with the City Budget Officer, to include in the annual budget sufficient appropriation for insurance expenses to ensure that all insurable properties are covered with the General Service Insurance Fund.
observations.managementAction: Insurance of insurable property was facilitated. Coordination was made. The City Budget Officer included appropriation for insurance in the annual budget.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the: a. City Planning and Development Coordinator, in coordination with the BAC Chairperson, to effectively and judiciously plan the development projects to be included in the Annual Investment Program (AIP) by indicating specific time schedule for each procurement activity throughout the year; and b. City Engineer to provide status of each project as of March 15, 2024 to the Audit Team and conduct thorough detailed engineering activities in the planning of development projects to prevent issues encountered in prior year from recurring.
observations.managementAction: Coordination was made. The OIC-City Engineer stated they will closely coordinate with the CPDCO and continue to actively participate in regular inspections conducted by the City Project Monitoring and Evaluation Committee to ensure proper oversight and timely implementation of infrastructure projects.
observations.reason: Ongoing: Some projects still pending due to issues encountered in prior years and revisions in scope; monitoring and coordination ongoing.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the: a. City Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer to ensure that QRF is used exclusively for relief, rehabilitation and reconstruction programs in the event of actual calamity supported by declaration of state of calamity; and b. City Budget Officer to refrain from charging obligations against the budget for QRF if not supported with valid declaration of state of calamity.
observations.managementAction: QRF was used for its exclusive use. The City Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer to ensure QRF is used only for relief and reconstruction; City Budget Officer to refrain from charging obligations to QRF if not supported.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City HRMO to: a. Expedite filling-up of vacant plantilla positions and hiring of qualified personnel pursuant to CSC laws, rules and regulations; and b. Hire qualified personnel and promote qualified casual and JO personnel to permanent positions pursuant to approved Merit Selection Plan to lessen need to hire additional JO personnel.
observations.managementAction: Filling-up of plantilla positions was prioritized. The City HRMO filled-out 68 plantilla positions in 2024 pursuant to CSC approved Merit Selection Plan.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City HRMO to immediately adopt the Automated Teller Machine (ATM) payroll system to safeguard government funds from risk of loss or misapplication associated with the manual payroll system.
observations.managementAction: ATM payroll was adopted for the payment of salaries and wages of JO.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the: a. City General Services Officer to: • exhaust all actions to retrieve the documents necessary for the registration of the 779 parcels of land such as communicating with the donors/previous owners to execute Deed of Donation,etc.; • initiate and expedite the processing of the Transfer Certificate of Title (TCT) for the 779 lots held by the City of San Pedro in compliance with Section 39(2) of PD No. 1445 and Section148 of COA Circular No. 92-386 to protect the interest of the City from possible third-party claims and land disputes;and b. City Accountant to record in the books of accounts the noted land once the ownership documents are obtained by the City.
observations.managementAction: No action was taken by the office. The City GSO is still retrieving documents necessary for registration. The City Accountant is awaiting the submission of Deed of Donation and other documents to support the recording and recognition of the subject parcels of land.
observations.reason: No action was taken by the Management. The City General Services Officer is still retrieving necessary documents necessary for registration. The City Accountant is awaiting the submission of Deed of Donation and other documents to support the recording and recognition of the subject parcels of land.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to: a. strictly require the Accountable Officers (AOs) to liquidate their CAs with complete documentary requirements within the prescribed period; b. issue and send Updated demand letters to all AOs with unliquidated cash advances; and c. stop the practice of granting additional or new CAs to AOs with outstanding CAs. We also recommended that the City Mayor enforce and institute other actions such as withholding of salaries, and filing of administrative and criminal sanctions of/against AOs/employees with long and overdue CAs, pursuant to COA Circular No. 97-002.
observations.managementAction: The City Accountant already identified the variance totaling P4,805,819.63 out of P6,019,030.15 on the Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds accounts, which involve entries for the recognition of the amount to be transferred to Trust Fund pertaining to the 20 percent share in fire code fees from CYs 2018 to 2020 through GJ-401-2024-01. As to the remaining P1,213,210.55 discrepancy, the City Accounting Office is still tracing back records affecting the reciprocal accounts for further adjustment in the books of accounts. The City Treasurer specified that the present administration continuously reminds and mandates all AOs to liquidate all cash advances within the prescribed period. The City Accountant specified that of the cash advances totaling P77,242,189.19, P65,478,095.17 or 84.77%, have already been liquidated to date. Also, the City Accounting Office already sent demand letters dated March 21, 2024, to the AOs with unliquidated cash advances which were duly acknowledged by the AOs as evidenced by the submitted demand letters.
observations.reason: CAs totaling P77,242,189.19 were still not liquidated as of December 31, 2023 due to the inability of the AOs to timely liquidate within the prescribed period. No action was taken to fully stop granting additional CAs to AOs with outstanding CAs due to exigencies of programs. Withholding of salaries of AOs with long outstanding CAs will be implemented in CY 2024.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor enforce the immediate completion of the project if found to be feasible, otherwise, terminate the contract and impose necessary action thereafter.
observations.managementAction: The project monitoring team assessed the status of the project. The implementation of the project was put in status quo. Management is still finalizing the termination of the contract.
observations.reason: No action was taken by the Management yet. The City had to determine whether to terminate the contract or continue to implement; the project was put in status quo and Management is still finalizing the termination of the contract.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the concerned contractors to immediately refund the excessive contract cost listed in the attached Annex J, otherwise a Notice of Disallowance shall be issued. We also recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Engineer to ensure that the approved Program of Works (POW) and Detailed Estimates of any project does not include unnecessary items/works to prevent the incurrence of excessive Approved Budget for the Contract (ABC)/contract cost, in compliance with Section 36 of RA No. 9184.
observations.managementAction: No action was taken by the Management. The COA Technical Audit Division still noted that the contract costs of three implemented infrastructure projects totaling P10,122,663.41 were excessive by a total of P745,103.35 due to overestimation and/or unnecessary inclusion of items/works in CY 2023. Concerned contractors were notified to refund or rectify the noted transactions. This is subject to further audit actions as warranted.
observations.reason: No action was taken by the Management. Concerned contractors are not coordinating with the City Engineer. The COA Technical Audit Division still noted the excessive costs and issuance of Notices of Disallowance shall be undertaken.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the concerned contractors to immediately rectify the noted deficiencies, otherwise demand the refund of the equivalent cost. We also recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Engineer to ensure that all projects are strictly implemented based on the approved POW.
observations.managementAction: No action was taken by the Management. Management will issue final demand letters to the concerned contractors ordering rectification of the noted deficiencies. The approved POW is now being strictly implemented.
observations.reason: No action was taken by the Management yet. Concerned contractors are not coordinating with the City Engineer.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Engineer and the City Planning and Development Coordinator to: a. effectively and judiciously plan the priority development projects to be included in the Annual Investment Program (AIP) by considering its feasibility and necessity and which could be strictly implemented for a given timeframe; b. conduct thorough detailed engineering activities, including coordination with other government agencies such as the Department of Public Works and Highways and the barangays, in the planning of development projects; and c. ensure the full implementation of development projects to help achieve the desirable socio-economic and environmental outcomes of the City.
observations.managementAction: City Mayor issued Executive Order No. 23, series of 2023 reorganizing the City Project Monitoring and Evaluation Committee and its Technical Working Group which shall coordinate, monitor and evaluate the implementation of local development projects. The City Engineer explained that priority was given to completion of previous years' projects; utilization rate for said year reached 79.35 percent as of December 31, 2023. AIP for CY 2025 was not yet submitted to the Audit Team.
observations.reason: 39 infrastructure projects with total appropriated amount of P216,822,688.74 remained partially and/or not implemented as of December 31, 2023. The reprogram and delay was due to issues encountered in the implementation of prior year’s projects, revision/changes in projects upon reassessment, and delay in the procurement process. The AIP for CY 2025 was not yet submitted to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to continue reviewing all the accounting entries that affect both accounts and make necessary adjustments, if warranted, to ensure the fair presentation of the accounts in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The City Accounting Office has already identified and corrected errors amounting to P5,020,492.22 affecting the reciprocal accounts. The City Accountant is still tracing back prior years’ transactions affecting these accounts for further adjustment.
observations.reason: The City Accountant already identified the variance totaling P5,020,492.22 out of P11,039,522.37. As of December 31, 2023, the remaining variance of P6,019,030.15 was still unreconciled. The City Accounting Office is still tracing back records affecting the reciprocal accounts for further adjustment in the books of accounts.

observations.recommendation: a. We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the concerned City Officials to immediately implement the remaining projects funded by NGAs, to ensure that the benefits that could be derived therefrom be effectively and efficiently delivered to its constituents. b. We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the concerned City Officials to return the remaining or unutilized balances of fund transfers to their respective Source Agencies.
observations.managementAction: Fund transfer from the Bureau of Treasury amounting to P17,848,668.00 was already utilized during CY 2022 for the project "Redevelopment of the City Plaza". The City Accountant explained there is an ongoing reconciliation with TESDA Regional Office No. IV-A relative to unexpended balances and agreed to return unexpended balances once reconciliation concluded. Some unutilized funds were returned; outstanding amounts remain subject to reconciliation.
observations.reason: Three PPAs funded by NGAs were not fully implemented within the prescribed period. Unutilized funds amounting to P2,660,972.94 were not yet returned to Source Agencies; P667,697.00 pertains to unliquidated cash advances of the then Disbursing Officer and remains unreturned. Ongoing reconciliation with TESDA and other agencies is pending.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to draw a Journal Entry Voucher to drop from the books of accounts the sold/condemned property upon disposal.
observations.managementAction: The City General Services Officer submitted the complete IIRUP for all unserviceable property subject for disposal. The Appraisal and Disposal Committee was constituted only January 9, 2024 and actual disposal will be facilitated immediately.
observations.reason: Actual disposal of the requested unserviceable PPEs was still not facilitated as of report date. The Appraisal and Disposal Committee was newly constituted and disposal actions are pending.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
a. City Administrator to prepare memorandum for the:
• Proper planning, scheduling and strategic implementation of PPAs drawn
through cash advances of the concerned offices to limit the granting of
cash advances in the latter part of the year; and
• Strict enforcement on the granting and liquidation of cash advances
pursuant to COA Circular No. 97-002 and Section 89 of PD No. 1445.
b. City Accountant and City Treasurer to:
• Send updated demand letters to all AOs with unliquidated cash advances;
and
• Stop the practice of granting additional or new cash advances to AOs with
outstanding cash advances unless their previous cash advances are fully
liquidated.
We also recommended that the City Mayor enforce and institute other actions
such as withholding of salaries of AOs and filing administrative and criminal
sanctions in case of failure to liquidate/settle their cash advances within the
prescribed period and for those AOs who failed to settle their long and overdue
cash advances under COA Circular No. 97-002, as warranted.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to:
a. Provide Allowance for Impairment for the assessed/evaluated non-collectible
receivables, in compliance with the pertinent provisions of IPSAS 29; and
b. Facilitate request for write-off and conduct necessary procedures for proper
disposition of dormant accounts as prescribed in Item 7.0 of COA Circular No.
2023-008.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to:
a. Identify recording errors by tracing back all transactions from CYs 2015 to
2023 affecting the Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds accounts
and effect the necessary adjustments to correct the reported balances of the
reciprocal accounts in the financial statements; and
b. Ensure that transaction which affects two or more funds are recorded
simultaneously, using the proper reciprocal accounts, in the corresponding
books of accounts to prevent the incurrence of unreconciled differences, in
compliance with Section 111 of PD No. 1445 and the RCA for LGUs.

We recommended that the City Mayor, in coordination with the Sangguniang
Panlungsod, expedite the preparation of the documents relative to the noted
donations.
We also recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
a. Appraisal and Disposal Committee to provide the fair market values or
appraisal values of the donated property if there are no declared values in the
donation documents;
b. City General Services Officer to issue PAR/ARE and install property stickers
for the donated property once donation documents are secured;
c. City Accountant to recognize the donated property in the books of accounts
upon receipt of the donation documents; and
d. Concerned offices to ensure proper coordination are in place from the receipt
until recording of the donated property to ensure appropriate recognition and
property accountability.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Engineer to:
a. Immediately submit the noted lacking documents to the Office of the Auditor to
facilitate further technical inspection and evaluation of the COA Technical
Audit Division, otherwise pertinent transactions will be suspended in audit;
and
b. Ensure that necessary supporting documents are submitted simultaneously
with the copy of perfected contracts to facilitate timely and systematic review
and evaluation of the projects.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
a. City General Services Officer to submit a complete IIRUP for all unserviceable
property;
b. Appraisal and Disposal Committee to facilitate the immediate disposal of the
noted unserviceable property, in accordance with the existing rules and
regulations; and
c. City Accountant to draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to drop from the books
of accounts the sold/condemned property upon disposal.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the BAC Chairperson to evaluate the
proper mode of procurement to attain the most practical, economical and
advantageous mode for the procuring entity, in compliance with the pertinent
provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.
We also recommended that the City Mayor direct the Head of the City
Procurement Office to ensure that copies of properly filled-out POs and its
supporting documents, were furnished to the Office of the Auditor within five
working days from its execution, in compliance with the pertinent provisions of
COA Circular No. 2009-001.

We recommended that the City Mayor enforce the withholding of salary of the then
Disbursing Officer with unliquidated cash advances amounting to P667,697.00
without prejudice to the institution of other sanctions pursuant to COA Circular
No. 97-002.
We also recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant and City
Treasurer to:
a. Facilitate the immediate return of the noted unexpended balances to TESDA
Regional Office No. IV-A; and
b. Ensure that unexpended balances of fund transfers from
completed/implemented programs are returned to the source agencies in
accordance with the pertinent provisions of the MOA and COA Circular No. 94-
013.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the concerned contractors to
immediately rectify the noted deficiencies, otherwise, demand the refund of the
equivalent excessive cost thereof.
We also recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Engineer to ensure that
the approved POW and Detailed Estimates of any project are not overestimated
and/or do not include unnecessary items/works to prevent the incurrence of
excessive ABC and contract cost, in compliance with Section 36 of RA No. 9184.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City General Services Officer to
immediately facilitate the insurance of the noted properties with the GSIS under
the General Service Insurance Fund.
We also recommended that the City Mayor, in coordination with the City Budget
Officer, include in the annual budget sufficient appropriation for insurance
expenses to ensure that all insurable properties are covered by property
insurance with the General Service Insurance Fund of the GSIS, in compliance
with the pertinent provisions of RA No. 656 and COA Circular No. 92-390.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
a. City Planning and Development Coordinator, in coordination with the
Chairperson of the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC), to effectively and
judiciously plan the priority development projects to be included in the AIP by
indicating specific time schedule for each procurement activity all throughout
the year while considering its feasibility and necessity and which could be
strictly implemented for a given timeframe; and
b. City Engineer to:
• Provide status of each project as of March 15, 2024 to the Audit Team; and
• Conduct thorough detailed engineering activities in the planning of
development projects and ensure that issues encountered in prior year will
be prevented to recur for timely implementation/completion of the project
pursuant to the pertinent provisions of RA No. 9184.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the Chairperson of the Bids and
Awards Committee (BAC), in coordination with the City Engineer, to ensure that
copies of the contracts and all its essential supporting documents are furnished
to the Office of the Auditor within five working days from its execution, in
compliance with pertinent provisions of COA Circular No. 2009-001.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
a. City Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer to ensure that QRF is
used exclusively for relief, rehabilitation and reconstruction programs and
projects in the event of actual calamity and supported by declaration of state
of calamity either by the President or local sanggunian; and
b. City Budget Officer to refrain from charging obligations against the budget for
QRF if not supported with valid declaration of state of calamity.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Human Resources and
Management Officer to prioritize the filling-up of vacant plantilla positions,
especially those which are vital in the delivery of basic services, by:
a. Expediting promotion of qualified permanent personnel pursuant to CSC laws,
rules and regulations; and
b. Hiring of qualified applicants or promoting qualified casual and JO personnel to
permanent positions pursuant to CSC approved Merit Selection Plan to
lessen the need to hire additional JO personnel.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Human Resources
Management Officer to immediately adopt the ATM payroll system to safeguard
government funds from risk of loss or misapplication associated with the manual
payroll system.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Treasurer to furnish the City Accountant of a duly certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year as basis in setting up the RPT and SET Receivables and Deferred RPT and SET Income accounts at the beginning of the year based on RPT Account Register/Taxpayer’s Index Card provided by the City Treasurer, in compliance with Section 20 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I.
observations.managementAction: Proper set-up of the beginning balance of RPT and SET Receivables based on RPT Account Register/Taxpayer’s Index Card provided by the City Treasurer is already being observed by the City Accountant.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer to conduct collection strategies and tax campaign program to improve collection of tax delinquencies and prepare an action plan, and conduct tax caravans and continuous reminder through print and social media platforms.
observations.managementAction: Action plan and collection strategies were already executed by the City Treasurer including, but not limited to, issuance of Notices of Delinquencies on the majority of real property owners, condonation of payments on interest for real property taxpayers, conduct of tax caravans and print & social media campaigns.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the: a. Inventory Committee to complete the physical inventory activities set-forth in the Physical Inventory Plan (PIP); b. City General Services Officer to update their records and submit the RPCPPE based on the results of the one-time cleansing activities to the Office of the Auditor; and c. City Accountant to update their records and prepare necessary adjustments in the books of accounts relative to the PPE items found at the station and non-existing/missing PPE items based on the results of the one-time cleansing activities. We also recommended that the City Mayor direct the City General Services Officer and City Accountant to strictly comply with all the rules and regulations on the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2020-006 to come up with the reconciled and reliable balances of PPE.
observations.managementAction: The conduct of the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances was successfully completed by the City in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006. The Inventory Committee activities set-forth in the PIP has already completed. RPCPPE on the results of the one-time cleansing activities was already submitted by the City General Services Officer. Accounting records has already updated based on the results of the one-time cleansing activities of the City.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to: a. provide an Allowance for Impairment for the noted receivables, in compliance with the pertinent provisions of IPSAS 29; and b. conduct necessary procedures to assess and secure/produce all relevant documents enumerated under Item 8.1 of COA Circular No. 2016-005 to support the filing of request for authority to write-off from the COA of undocumented dormant receivables or where settlement/collectability could no longer be ascertained.
observations.managementAction: The City Accountant already prepared the necessary adjusting entries to provide Allowance for Impairment for the assessed/evaluated non-collectible receivables through Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) No. 101-2024-03-005 dated March 31, 2024. Furthermore, he already identified three deceased AOs with dormant receivables with corresponding attached death certificates, for application for request for write-off pursuant to COA Circular No. 2023-008 upon completion of all documentary requirements. Few individuals who were already deceased were initially identified by the City Accounting Office. Management is still in the process of obtaining documents to facilitate the process.
observations.reason: Still on the process of obtaining documents required in the process of write off. Management initially identified three deceased AOs with dormant receivables with corresponding attached death certificates, for application for request for write-off pursuant to COA Circular No. 2023-008 upon completion of all documentary requirements.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Treasurer to conduct collection strategies and tax campaign program to improve collection of tax delinquencies and prepare an action plan, and conduct tax caravans and continuous reminder through print and social media platforms.
observations.managementAction: Action plan and collection strategies were already executed by the City Treasurer including issuance of Notices of Delinquencies on the majority of real property owners, condonation of payments on interest for real property taxpayers, conduct of tax caravans and print & social media campaigns.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor make representations with the Sangguniang Panlungsod for the enactment of an Ordinance relative to the updated Schedule of Fair Market Values (FMVs) of real property by which assessment level on real property be updated.
observations.managementAction: City Ordinance No. 2023-49 dated December 27, 2023 has already passed prescribing the updated Schedule of FMVs and assessment level on real property.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the GAD Focal Person to: a. submit written justification on the partial/non-implementation of 25 PPAs; and b. formulate feasible and attainable GAD PPAs that could be implemented for a given period and ensure that PPAs are accomplished in order to maximize the utilization of the GAD fund.
observations.managementAction: The GAD Focal Person submitted written justification on the partial/non-implementation of PPAs which the Audit Team found sufficient. The City was able to maximize the utilization of the GAD fund for CY 2023.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to maintain a complete and updated SLCs for each type of supplies and materials and ensure conduct of complete physical count and proper recognition of inventory transactions.
observations.managementAction: The City Accountant completed the updating of SLCs.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to prepare and maintain complete subsidiary records of each special account. Accordingly, at the end of each year, prepare Trial Balance and Financial Statements of each special account in the General Fund, in compliance with Section 110, Chapter 6 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I.
observations.managementAction: The City Accountant completed the necessary steps and recorded the transactions. City Ordinance on the establishment of the economic enterprises was enacted.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the CGSO and the City
Accountant to facilitate the immediate completion and observe proper
maintenance of the Property Cards and PPE Ledger Cards, respectively, per
category of PPE for all property of the City, in compliance with Sections 114 and
119 of MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the:

a. City Accountant and CGSO to evaluate and analyze the causes of
discrepancies in the PPE accounts, and reconcile their records based on
appropriate documents and make adjustments, if necessary; and
b. Inventory Committee to comply with the guidelines and procedures in the
conduct of the One-Time Cleansing of PPE account balances as provided in
COA Circular No. 2020-006.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the Inventory Committee to
analyze and investigate the reasons behind the missing or unlocated properties,
and strictly follow the disposition procedures for non-existing/missing PPEs as
prescribed under Paragraph 7 of COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31,
2020.

We also recommended that the City Mayor continue directing the identified AOs
to file a request for relief from property accountability, if the loss was not due to
negligence, following the procedures under Section 73 of PD No. 1445,
otherwise, require payment of the missing property equivalent to its monetary
value.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the:

a. City Engineer to provide assistance to the City Accountant for the inspection
as to the current status of the completed projects transferred to RPI; and
b. City Accountant to gather all related and available documents and records,
and thereafter, record/book the Public Infrastructure Assets that were
previously transferred to the RPI with the corresponding accumulated
depreciation, by using the appropriate accounts prescribed in the Revised
Chart of Accounts for Local Government Units (LGUs), in compliance with
COA Circular No. 2016-004.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant and City
Treasurer to:

a. require the AOs to properly and sufficiently document all the transactions
involved in their cash advances, and immediately submit all the necessary
attachments to their liquidation reports, subsequently, the City Accountant
to immediately record the liquidation in the books through Journal Entry
Voucher (JEV);
b. continue sending demand letters to all AOs with unliquidated cash
advances including those AOs who are already out of service, resigned,
and/or retired;
c. stop the practice of granting additional or new cash advances to AOs with
outstanding cash advances unless their previous cash advances are fully
liquidated;
d. request for AO’s authorization (in affidavit form) to deduct all financial
obligations from Terminal Benefit as required in COA Circular No. 2012-001
for AOs who are no longer in the service, resigned and or retired;
e. fast track the completion of the supporting documents to support the
request for authority to the write-off of accountability for deceased AOs
following the guidelines, procedures, and documents required under COA
Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016;
f. monitor and refrain from granting cash advances to employees beyond
the maximum cash accountability of their bond; otherwise, ensure that the
AOs are properly and sufficiently bonded; and
g. strictly enforce the withholding of salaries of AOs for failure to
liquidate/settle their cash advances within the prescribed period, in
accordance with Section 100 of PD No. 1445, otherwise, enforce
administrative and criminal sanctions to all AOs who failed to settle their
long-overdue cash advances, pursuant to COA Circular No. 97-002.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to continue
reviewing all the accounting entries that affect both accounts and make
necessary adjustments, if warranted, to ensure the fair presentation of the
accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the concerned City Officials to
immediately implement the remaining projects funded by NGAs, to ensure that
the benefits that could be derived therefrom be effectively and efficiently
delivered to its constituents.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the concerned City officials to
return the remaining or unutilized balances of fund transfers to their respective
SAs to finance other government projects.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the concerned officials and
employees to:

a. monitor and control the fuel consumption and use of government vehicles
by requiring all the drivers to properly prepare and submit the serially
numbered and duly accomplished Drivers’ Trip Tickets and Monthly Report
of Official Travels using the prescribed format;
b. require the repair of all the odometer and fuel gauge of all serviceable
vehicles, otherwise, the distance traveled should be estimated based on the
estimate of distance from point of origin to point of destination; and
c. strictly enforce the City Mayor’s Memorandum Order OM-2021-06-205 dated
June 15, 2021, and Item (B)(2) of the Manual on Audit for Fuel Consumption
of Government Motor Vehicles as prescribed under COA Circular No. 77-61.

We recommended that the City Mayor:

a. require the concerned Contractors to implement or refund the deficiencies
noted in Appendix L, otherwise, will be disallowed in audit; and
b. direct the City Engineer to carefully inspect and ensure that all projects are
completely implemented as per approved POW.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the Head, City Planning and
Development Office and the City Engineer to:

a. judiciously plan and prioritize the implementation of feasible, practicable
and beneficial projects within the City; and
b. take immediate actions to address the issues and concerns of the residents
of the Barangay San Roque particularly the canal/drainage system located
at Kalye Pogi and Mendoza Street.

We recommended that the City Mayor and the concerned Department Heads
review Executive Order No. 19, series of 2020, and make necessary revisions to
ensure its harmony and compliance with the existing laws, rules, and
regulations.

Moreover, the City Mayor instruct the:

a. Internal Audit Office to perform its audit functions as necessary to ensure
compliance with the policies and procedures in the requisition, acceptance,
inspection, and issuance of supplies and property and that appropriate
internal controls are effectively operating; and
b. City General Services Office, all Departments, and end-users to strictly
observe the accounting policies and procedures for supplies or property as
provided under Chapter 7 of MNGAs for LGUs, Volume I to eliminate
duplicate processes, save time and resources and avoid possible errors.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant in coordination with the City Engineering Office, City General Services Office and other concerned offices to: a. exert extra effort to locate the City’s Registry of Public Infrastructure, otherwise, gather relevant information and other related documents to provide an accurate list of Infrastructure Assets and its corresponding amount; and b. record back to the books the Infrastructure Assets that were previously transferred to the RPI with their corresponding accumulated depreciation by using the appropriate accounts prescribed in the Revised Chart of Accounts for LGUs and furnish the Office of the Auditor with the copy of the Journal Entry Voucher (JEV).
observations.managementAction: The concerned department heads will convene to discuss the City’s Registry of Public Infrastructure. Once information and other relevant documents were gathered, the City Accountant will provide the necessary JEV and furnish a copy to the Office of the Auditor.
observations.reason: The City was not able to record back the said Infrastructure Assets as the RPI was not readily available. The City Accountant is coordinating with the City Engineering Office and City General Services Office to gather information and other related documents that can be used as source in recording the said assets in the books of the City.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Engineer to provide the City Accountant with the complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects to facilitate the proper disclosure of the road network system in the Notes to Financial Statements by the City Accountant.
observations.managementAction: The City Accountant reviewed the composition of the Road Networks accounts and Construction in Progress accounts and coordinated to the City Engineering Office and City General Services Office for the documents and records needed to properly maintain it for as inspection and acceptance of the projects and statement of work accomplished.
observations.reason: The details of the Road Network account were not disclosed due to ongoing inventory of the road networks.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to coordinate with the Provincial Accountant of Laguna to address the discrepancies in the reciprocal accounts Due from LGUs and Due to LGUs and make the necessary adjustments.
observations.managementAction: The City Accountant coordinated with the Provincial Accountant of Laguna. Reconciliation on the noted items was started and the City submitted documents to Provincial Accountant to validate the differences. The City was able to reconcile amounts and continued to validate remaining items.
observations.reason: There were ongoing reconciliations and the City did not complete the reconciliation as of the report date due to pending validation from the Provincial office.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended thatthe City Mayorinstruct the:

a. City GSO to conduct complete physical inventory of all the City’s PPE and
update itsRPCPPE; and

b. City Accountant and the City GSO to reconcile their records and
thoroughly evaluate and analyze the causes of discrepancies for all the
PPE accounts and make necessary adjustments in their records to
ascertain the accuracy of the PPE balances reported in the Financial
Statements.

We recommended thatthe City Mayorinstruct the:

a. City Accountant and City Engineer to reconcile the on-going projects
reported in their respective CIP schedules and GPPA Reports to reflect the
correct balance of theCIPaccount; and

b. City Accountant to fast track the retrieval of documents for completed
projects as basis for the reclassification of CIP accounts to its proper PPE
accounts and make necessary adjustments, if warranted; otherwise, seek
the assistance of the City Engineer and Procurement Office for the copy of
Statement of Work Accomplished, Certificate of Project Completion and
billing from the suppliers; and the City Engineer to provide assistance to
the City Accountant in determining the unaccounted CIP accounts from the
previous years.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant in
coordination with the City Engineering Office, City General Services Office and
other concernedoffices to:

a. exert extra effort to locate the City’s Registry of Public Infrastructure,
otherwise, gather relevant information and other related documents to
provide an accurate list of Infrastructure Assets and its corresponding
amount; and

b. record back to the books the Infrastructure Assets that were previously
transferred to the RPI with their corresponding accumulated depreciation
by using the appropriate accounts prescribed in the Revised Chart of
Accounts for LGUs and furnish the Office of the Auditor with the copy of
the Journal Entry Voucher (JEV).

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to review all
the accounting entries that affect both accounts, determine the cause/s of the
discrepancy between their balances, after which make the necessary
adjustments to bring into agreement the balances of the reciprocal accounts to
ensure fairpresentation of the accountsinthe Financial Statements.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant and City
Treasurer to:

a. require the AOs to properly document all the transactions involved in their
cash advances and immediately submit all the necessary attachments to
their liquidation report, otherwise, stop granting additional cash advances
to those officials, employees or AOs with unliquidated cash advances; and
tostrictly monitor thegranting, utilization and liquidationof cash advances;

b. prepare follow-up demand letters to all AOs with unliquidated cash
advances including those AOs whohave retired oralready out of service;

c. initiate the request for authority to write-off of accountability for deceased
AOs following the guidelines, procedures and documents required under
COACircular No. 2016-005 dated December19, 2016;

d. request for AO’s authorization (in affidavit form) to deduct all financial
obligations from Terminal Benefit as required in COA Circular No. 2012-001
forAOs whoare no longerintheservice; and

e. strictly enforce the withholding of salaries of AOs for failure to
liquidate/settle their cash advances within the prescribed period in
accordance withSection 100 ofPD No. 1445.

Likewise, enforce administrative and criminal sanctions to all AOs who failed
to settle their long overdue cash advances, pursuant to COA Circular No. 97-
002dated February 10, 1997.

We recommended thatthe City Mayorinstruct the:


City Accountant to:

a. maintain a complete and updated Supplies Ledger Cards for each type
of supplies andmaterials; and
b. record the issuances based on the Summary of Supplies and Materials
Issued (SSMI) instead of theRIS; and


CityGeneral Services Officer(CGSO) andother concerned personnel to:

a. monitor and record the receipts and issuances of all types of
inventories before its distribution to the end-users and maintain Stock
Cards foreach type ofinventories/supplies;

b. prepare and submit monthly the SSMI to the City Accountant for the
recording of issuances; and

c. prepare and submit the RPCI to report the physical count of
supplies/materials bytype ofinventory as of agiven date.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant and City
Treasurer to transfer the fund received by the City for the 20 percent share of
the LGU from the 50 percent incremental collection from Value Added Tax
totaling P2,626,362.48 from the Trust Fund to the General Fund and make the
necessary adjustment intheirrecords.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to prepare
and maintain complete subsidiary records of each special account.

Accordingly, at the end of each year, prepare Trial Balance and Financial
Statements of each special account in the General Fund, in compliance with
Section110, Chapter 6 of theMNGAS for LGUs,Volume I.

We recommended thatthe City Mayordirect the:

a. Appraisal and Disposal Committee to immediately undertake an appraisal
of these unserviceable properties and prepare a corresponding report
thereon; and facilitate the immediate disposal of the unserviceable
properties in accordance with existing rules and regulations to prevent
further deterioration due to continuous exposure to elements thereof that
woulddecrease thesalvage value of theproperties;

b. City General Services Officer to submit to COA Auditor a request for
disposal of all unserviceable property together with the Inventory and
Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP), Appraisal Report and
other required documents for appropriate action; and

c. City Accountant to draw a JEV to drop from the books of accounts the
sold/condemned properties upon disposal.

We recommended thatthe City Mayorinstruct the:

a. City Accountant and City Treasurer to furnish copies of the Report of
Collections and Deposits, Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) and credit memo to
the OIC-City Budget as bases in the preparation of the Registry of Cash
Donations and Utilization (RCDU); and submit quarterly report on the
Receipt and Utilization of Cash Donations within ten working days after the
endof each quarter;

b. City GSO, in coordination with the City DRRMO, CSWD and Office of the
Mayor, to submit the Summary/List of Donations Received, Distributed and
Balances supported with (1) acknowledgement receipts of the donation in-
kind, (2) proof of receipt by and distribution to the beneficiaries, and (3)
inventory of remaining undistributed items, if any, within ten working days
aftertheendof each quarter; andmake anyadjustments, ifwarranted; and

c. All concerned City Officials to strictly adhere with all the provisions of COA
Circular Nos. 2014-002 and 2020-009 to properly account and report the
receipt and utilization of all cashand in-kind donations from local and
foreign sources.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer to prepare an
aging of real property tax dues of delinquent taxpayers and strongly enforce
collection remedies allowed by the pertinent provisions of RA No. 7160,
particularly the sale of delinquent properties through public auction to ensure
higher revenues to finance the City’s operations and implementation of
various programs andprojects.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Engineer, together with
the City Planning andDevelopmentOfficerand CityAccountant to:

a. prepare and submit within the prescribed period a comprehensive list of all
on-going and to be implemented government PPAs as mandated by
Section122 of PD No. 1445 andSection 2.1of COACircular No. 2013-004;

b. prepare and maintain a monthly monitoring report containing all the
necessary details and information of all the PPAs for ready reference in
updating the Project Status and in preparation of the quarterly reports as
required under Section 3.2 of theCOACircularNo. 2013-004; and

c. notify the Audit Team within ten days after the award of the infrastructure
project or before the start of the program/activity that the appropriate
project signboards and/or public notices are already posted pursuant to
Section3.1 ofCOACircular No. 2013-004.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Treasurer to adopt
internal control measures through regular monitoring of CIB balances to avoid
incurring unnecessary expenditures in the form of bank service charges and
refundthe P5,400.00 bankservice charge.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant and City
Treasurer to:

a. immediately send notice to the concerned payees of unclaimed checks and
submit thereafter the related DVs uponthe release of checks;

b. hire full time Accounting personnel or assign additional personnel in the
City Treasurer’s Office and/or Office of the City Accountant to facilitate the
timely preparation andsubmission of needed documents and reports;and

c. observe timeliness in the submission of reports including DVs and its
supporting documents, liquidation reports, payrolls and official receipts to
the Audit Team within ten days of the ensuing month in compliance with
COACircular Nos. 95-006 and2009-006.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to strictly
comply with the prompt submission of Financial Statements, Trial Balances
and other pertinent documents/supporting schedules as mandated under
Section 347 of RA No. 7160, Section 4.1 of COA Circular No. 2010-001 and COA
Circular 2016-004. Moreover, evaluate the necessity of hiring additional
accounting personnel to assist in the reconciliation of accounts and in the
preparation of financial reports.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the:
a. City GSO to conduct complete physical inventory of all the City’s PPE and update its RPCPPE; and
b. City Accountant and the City GSO to reconcile their records and thoroughly evaluate and analyze the causes of discrepancies for all the PPE accounts and make necessary adjustments in their records to ascertain the accuracy of the PPE balances reported in the Financial Statements.
observations.managementAction:
Management’s Comment:
The GSO will regularly and completely conduct the physical inventory of all the City’s property and the reports will be reconciled with the Accounting records.
Audit Team’s Rejoinder:
The Audit Team will monitor the City’s compliance with the audit recommendations.
observations.reason: Due to the resignation from office of one of its members and the Inventory Committee still performed the functions needed for the team. There were still unreconciled balances noted between the Accounting and GSO records. The Inventory Committee started the investigation for the causes of missing and unlocated property and will identify the corresponding accountable employees for proper action.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to draw a JEV debiting Accounts Payable and crediting Construction in Progress to correct the balance of the accounts.
observations.managementAction: The City did not reconstitute the Inventory Committee, however, the Inventory Committee still performed the functions needed for the team. The City Accounting Office already submitted Journal Entry Voucher Nos. 101-2020-08-01 and 221-2020-08-01 effecting the recommended adjusting entries. Adherence to the guidelines stated will be strictly followed by the Management.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to review all the accounting entries that affect both accounts, determine the cause/s of the discrepancy between their balances, after which make the necessary adjustments to bring into agreement the balances of the reciprocal accounts to ensure fair presentation of the accounts in the Financial Statements.
observations.managementAction: The City Accountant reviewed and corrected some errors in recording of the two accounts which decreased the unreconciled difference from prior year. The City Accountant will continue to review and reconcile the transactions affecting these accounts and make necessary adjustments.
observations.reason: There are still uncorrected errors in previous years' recorded transactions and review of the transactions pertaining to the accounts is still on-going.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Treasurer to furnish the City Accountant at the beginning of the year a duly certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount collectible for the year which will serve as basis to draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) in recording the debit to Real Property Tax Receivable/Special Education Tax Receivable and credit to Deferred Real Property Tax Income/Deferred Special Education Tax. We also recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to reconcile the RPT/SET Receivables accounts with the City Treasurer’s list of RPT/SET delinquencies and make necessary adjustments, if any, to present fairly the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The City Treasurer furnished the RPT/SET collectibles for CY 2020 to the City Accountant and reconciliations were performed on the accounts of RPT and SET Receivables. Said accounts were reviewed and adjusted accordingly.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to draw a Journal Entry Voucher to adjust the account in the Trust Fund books by debiting Trust Liability – DRRM account and crediting Due to Other Funds totaling P2,582,020.45 and to adjust the General Fund books by debiting Due from Other Funds and crediting Prior Period Adjustment totaling P2,582,020.45. We also recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant and City Treasurer to review the records affecting DRRMF and transfer the unutilized DRRM funds to special trust fund.
observations.managementAction: The City Accounting Office submitted Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) under the General Fund books (JEV No. 401-2020-06-01) and Trust Fund books (JEV No. CDRRM-2020-08-01) effecting the adjustments. The City Accounting Office started collecting the documents needed for the request for write-off and is validating their receivable accounts.
observations.reason: There were discrepancies noted and the City accountant is still in the process of reconciling the balances from previous years. The differences might be due to prior years' entries and ongoing validation; some balances remain incomplete due to ongoing retrieval of old records.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to: a. maintain a complete and updated Supplies Ledger Cards for each type of supplies and materials; and b. record the issuances based on the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) instead of the RIS; and to instruct the City General Services Officer (CGSO) and other concerned personnel to: a. monitor and record the receipts and issuances of all types of inventories before its distribution to the end-users and maintain Stock Cards for each type of inventories/supplies; b. prepare and submit monthly the SSMI to the City Accountant for the recording of issuances; and c. prepare and submit the RPCI to report the physical count of supplies/materials by type of inventory as of a given date.
observations.managementAction: The City Accountant will maintain a Supplies Ledger Cards for each type of supplies and materials and will record the issuance based on the SSMI. The City GSO is amenable with the audit observations and recommendations and will exert efforts to address the matter and recommend to other departments to identify a point person to do the recording of receipt and issuance of supplies in the RIS. All the RIS will be consolidated and SSMI will be submitted to the Accounting Office as basis for recording. The GSO will comply with the semestral submission of RPCI in accordance with the provided guidelines.
observations.reason: The City Accountant was not able to fully maintain Supplies Ledger Cards due to voluminous transactions and lack of accounting staff. The GSO indicated most inventories were directly issued to end-users and stock cards were not maintained for all items; SSMI preparation was only for some inventories and RPCI was only for office supplies.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to: a. review the documents on the guaranty deposits with various utility companies; b. exert extra efforts to expedite the retrieval of old records to establish the details of the reported Other Receivables and Due to NGAs; c. request for write-off of long overdue receivables to determine the present value and impairment loss; and d. liquidate the unsupported balances of Due to NGAs to the Bureau of the Treasury in case the details of the account cannot be determined.
observations.managementAction: The City Accountant reviewed all the MOAs with the possible source of the unutilized DRRM funds. The City Accountant started validation of its receivable accounts and is collecting documents needed for the request for write-off. Reconciliation on the outstanding receivable balances is ongoing; some balances were reduced while others remain incomplete due to ongoing retrieval.
observations.reason: Some unsupported balances remained due to incomplete documentation and ongoing validation and retrieval of old records; actual liquidation of balances to appropriate agencies has not been completed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the: a. City Accountant to ensure that all collections in the custody of the Treasury at year-end be accounted as Cash - Local Treasury and only record in Cash in Bank - Local Currency, Checking Account upon actual deposit of the collections; b. City Accountant to prepare accounting entries to take up the cancellation of stale checks in the books of accounts by debiting Cash-in-Bank, Local Currency, Current Account and crediting Accounts Payable in the amount of P13,966.85 and observe strict compliance to Section 13 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I; and c. City Treasurer to adjust the cashbook by adding the reversal of the outstanding check totaling P480.00 and send written notice to payees at least one month before a check becomes stale as required under Section 59 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I.
observations.managementAction: The City Accounting Office submitted JEV No. 401-2020-06-01 to recognize the cancellation of stale checks mentioned in the AOM. The Cashbook has been adjusted/updated based on the bank reconciliation statements. Written notices for maturing checks were sent as appropriate and the City Treasurer will frequently monitor the bank accounts to avoid unnecessary expenditures and bank charges.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to reclassify the accounts by debiting Prior Period Adjustment and crediting Biological Assets in the amount of P574,750.00 to present the correct balance of the account in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The City Accountant reclassified the Biological Assets account in the books of the City of San Pedro under Journal Entry Voucher Number 101-2020-08-01 dated August 13, 2020 and this was coordinated to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor: a. require the Local Finance Committee to study thoroughly the reasonableness of the projected income for the ensuing year; and b. require the City Treasurer, Head - Assessors Office, Head - BPLO and other concerned officials to conduct planned periodic review of its collection efficiency and the implementation of programs/projects/activities to keep track of revenue accounts that do not meet the revenue targets for appropriate remedial measures.
observations.managementAction: The Local Finance Committee held meeting to review and discuss the budget items and targets for the ensuing year. Monthly report of Actual and Budgeted income were being prepared by the Office of the City Accountant to monitor the collection efficiency.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant and City Treasurer to facilitate the immediate submission of the 271 DVs involving a total amount of P15,985,297.10 as well as the 18 DVs covering transactions from CYs 2015 to 2017 amounting to P135,161.10, otherwise the transactions shall be suspended/disallowed in audit. Henceforth, require submission of accounts within the first 10 days of the ensuing month as required by COA Circular Nos. 2009-006.
observations.managementAction: The City Treasurer regularly notifies, through text and call, the payees with unclaimed checks. Moreover, all disbursement vouchers, liquidation reports, RCD and official receipts were already forwarded to the City Accounting Office. The City Accountant will request for additional personnel to help meet the desired output on time.
observations.reason: Some DVs were submitted; however, 25 DVs remain unsubmitted and some supporting documents were still being located.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our previous recommendations that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant and City Treasurer to: a. stop granting cash advances to officials, employees or accountable officers with outstanding unliquidated cash advances and strictly monitor the granting and liquidation of cash advances; b. prepare follow-up demand letters to all AOs with unliquidated cash advances including those AOs who have retired or already out of service; c. initiate the request for authority to write-off of accountability for deceased AOs following COA Circular No. 2016-005; d. request for AO's authorization (in affidavit form) to deduct all financial obligations from Terminal Benefit as required in COA Circular No. 2012-001 for AOs who are no longer in the service; and e. strictly enforce the withholding of salaries of AOs for failure to liquidate/settle their cash advances within the prescribed period in accordance with Section 100 of PD No. 1445. Likewise, enforce administrative and criminal sanctions to all AOs who failed to settle their long overdue cash advances.
observations.managementAction: The City Accountant and the City Treasurer will strictly monitor the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances and make the necessary follow-up demand letters to the concerned AOs. He also added that a request for authority to write-off of accountability for deceased AOs will be prepared and submitted. The City will also enforce proper administrative and criminal sanctions to those AOs with long overdue CAs.
observations.reason: The City prepared and issued demand letters to AOs and filed an administrative/legal sanction against one accountable officer; however, several AOs still have outstanding CAs and not all cases were resolved.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to prepare and maintain complete subsidiary records of each special account. Accordingly, at the end of each year, prepare Trial Balance and Financial Statements of each special account in the General Fund, in compliance with Section 110, Chapter 6 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I.
observations.managementAction: The City Government of San Pedro is on the process of adopting a computerized accounting system. They are expecting that the system will be ready and operational by the first semester of CY 2022. With the new computerized accounting system, the City Accountant will be able to prepare and maintain complete subsidiary records of each special accounts and will prepare the corresponding trial balance and Financial Statements.
observations.reason: The city is migrating to a computerized accounting system; special accounts’ subsidiary ledgers were not yet prepared due to system unavailability and insufficient accounting personnel.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the concerned official to explain or justify why the MOA with TESDA was signed earlier than the approval date of the authority to sign the MOA and to coordinate with TESDA Region IV-A and DOLE Region IV-A, respectively, to put into writing any amendment and modifications from original MOA and submit the same to the Audit Team.
observations.managementAction: The SPTI Administrator and BUB Coordinator coordinated with TESDA Region IV-A and DOLE Region IV-A to put into writing any amendment and modification from the original MOA. The notarized MOA was sent to TESDA Region IV-A.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the Local Human Resource Management Office to adopt the ATM payroll system to safeguard government funds from risk of loss or misapplication associated with the manual payroll system.
observations.managementAction: The City implemented the Automated Payroll Services through Philippine Veterans Bank. The ATM payroll system was adopted and the necessary memorandum of agreement with PVB was approved.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to transfer the payroll preparation and computation, including the updating of remittance records, to the City Human Resource Management Office for proper internal control procedure.
observations.managementAction: The City Accountant and City Human Resource Management Office coordinated to improve duties and responsibilities. The City availed the ATM payroll mechanism for its job orders. The City will have close coordination for improvement of the payroll operations and requested additional personnel.
observations.reason: Some duties were transferred and ATM payroll used for some employees but full segregation of duties has not yet been enforced.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local School Board reprogram the fund to programs, projects and activities that are in compliance with the legal and regulatory requirements of the Special Education Fund as stated in Section 4 of DepEd-DBM-DILG JMC No. 1, s. 2017, otherwise the transactions shall be suspended/disallowed in audit; and include in the AIP programs, projects and activities that contribute to the attainment of socio-economic development and environmental outcomes and partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures in accordance with DILG and DBM JMC No. 2017-1.
observations.managementAction: The Local School Board reprogrammed the fund to other PPAs under SEF.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to transfer the unexpended balance of the QRF and LDRRMF-MOOE to the Special Trust Fund of the Trust Fund books, in compliance with COA Circular No. 2012-002.
observations.managementAction: The City Accountant started reconciling the records for the unexpended balances of the LDRRMF and transferred amounts where appropriate.
observations.reason: Reconciliation of the records is still ongoing; some unexpended balances were used for COVID-19 response and the transfers to Special Trust Fund were not yet fully completed as of report date.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the LDRRM Officer to submit a written explanation on charging the regular expenditures against the LDRRMF, otherwise the transactions shall be suspended/disallowed in audit, and to refrain from incurring and charging expenses which do not fall on the primary purpose of the calamity fund.
observations.managementAction: The CDRRM Officer assured that expenses which do not fall on the primary purpose of the calamity fund will no longer be incurred and charged against the fund.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the Local Disaster Risk and Reduction Management Council (LDRRMC) to review and update the LDRRMFIP by including all the PPAs of the previous years’ unexpended balances and any supplemental budget, in compliance with Section 5.1.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-002.
observations.managementAction: The LDRRMO will immediately submit a copy of the revised and complete LDRRMFIP to the Audit Team.
observations.reason: The council overlooked incorporation of previous years' unexpended balances due to being overwhelmed by the COVID-19 outbreak; the revised LDRRMFIP was not submitted as of report date.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor, in coordination with the City Planning and Development Coordinator (CPDC) and City Budget Officer, review and justify why such PPAs were included in the AIP funded by 20 percent Development fund and if not compliant, revert to General Fund or include PPAs in the AIP that comply with DILG and DBM JMC No. 2017-1.
observations.managementAction: The CPDC submitted written explanation and justification for the charges made in the 20 percent Development Fund. The CPDC assured that proper compliance will be observed and will include proper projects in the AIP.
